metadata.summary: BABOK organizes BA into six knowledge areas. Routing index for selecting applicable areas and sub-methodologies.

content.title: BA Knowledge Areas Overview
content.summary: BABOK organizes BA work into six knowledge areas. This routing index answers which apply and directs to sub-methodologies.

why.p: Without a framework map, BA activities appear as a random collection of tasks. Teams skip critical areas, produce inconsistent deliverables, and cannot explain which activities are appropriate for their project type. The BABOK knowledge area model provides a coherent structure that applies across waterfall, agile, and hybrid delivery contexts, while the five perspectives (Agile, BI, IT, Business Architecture, BPM) adapt it to specific domains.

list.item: Project kickoff where the BA has no plan and needs to select which knowledge areas apply.
Scoping a BA effort for a new feature or epic.
Auditing an in-flight project for BA knowledge area coverage gaps.
Onboarding a new BA or AI agent to a domain — load this first to get the routing map.
Routing inside a multi-agent BA pipeline to decide which sub-agent runs next.
You already know the specific technique needed — load the target methodology directly, skip the overview.
Small single-feature task with one stakeholder and known scope — overhead exceeds value.
Pure software design with no business requirements — use software-architect skills instead.
Agile teams with a working backlog and clear DoR/DoD — BABOK formalism adds friction.
Certification study — this file is operational context, not exam preparation.

section.name: knowledge-areas
section.p: All six knowledge areas: purpose, key tasks, outputs, and linked methodologies.

section.name: ka-1-planning-and-monitoring
section.p: KA-1: Business Analysis Planning and Monitoring
Purpose: plan how BA work will be done and track performance.

list.item: Plan BA Approach — define how BA will be performed; output: BA approach document.
Plan Stakeholder Engagement — identify and plan work with stakeholders; output: stakeholder engagement approach.
Plan Governance — define decision-making for requirements; output: governance approach.
Plan Information Management — define how BA info is stored and accessed; output: information management approach.
Identify Improvements — assess BA performance, recommend improvements.

section.p: Linked methodologies: ba-planning, stakeholder-analysis.

section.name: ka-2-elicitation-and-collaboration
section.p: KA-2: Elicitation and Collaboration
Purpose: draw out information from stakeholders and confirm results.

list.item: Prepare for Elicitation — understand scope, select techniques; output: elicitation activity plan.
Conduct Elicitation — draw out relevant information; output: elicitation results (unconfirmed).
Confirm Results — verify understanding with stakeholders; output: elicitation results (confirmed).
Communicate BA Information — ensure shared understanding.
Manage Collaboration — encourage stakeholders to work together.

section.p: Linked methodologies: elicitation-techniques. Techniques: interviews, workshops, observation, surveys, document analysis, prototyping, brainstorming, focus groups.

section.name: ka-3-requirements-lifecycle-management
section.p: KA-3: Requirements Lifecycle Management
Purpose: manage requirements through their full lifecycle.

list.item: Trace Requirements — ensure alignment at different levels; output: requirements (traced).
Maintain Requirements — keep accurate and consistent; output: requirements (maintained).
Prioritize Requirements — rank by importance; output: requirements (prioritized).
Assess Changes — evaluate proposed changes; output: change assessment.
Approve Requirements — obtain agreement; output: requirements (approved).

section.p: Linked methodologies: requirements-traceability, requirements-lifecycle, requirements-prioritization. Outputs: traceability matrix, requirements baseline, change impact analysis.

section.name: ka-4-strategy-analysis
section.p: KA-4: Strategy Analysis
Purpose: understand the current state and define the change strategy to achieve the future state.

list.item: Analyze Current State — understand existing environment; output: current state description.
Define Future State — define goals and objectives; output: future state description.
Assess Risks — identify uncertainties; output: risk assessment results.
Define Change Strategy — develop approach to achieve future state; output: change strategy.

section.p: Linked methodologies: strategy-analysis. Techniques: gap analysis, SWOT, business model canvas, capability assessment, risk analysis.

section.name: ka-5-requirements-analysis-and-design-definition
section.p: KA-5: Requirements Analysis and Design Definition
Purpose: structure, specify, model, verify, and validate requirements; define design options.

list.item: Specify and Model Requirements — output: requirements (specified/modeled).
Verify Requirements — ensure quality standards met; output: requirements (verified).
Validate Requirements — ensure alignment with business goals; output: requirements (validated).
Define Requirements Architecture — ensure requirements support each other.
Define Design Options — define solution approaches.
Analyze Value and Recommend — assess options, recommend solution.

section.p: Linked methodologies: use-case-modeling, user-story-mapping, data-analysis, business-process-analysis, decision-analysis, interface-analysis, requirements-validation, acceptance-criteria, requirements-documentation.

section.name: ka-6-solution-evaluation
section.p: KA-6: Solution Evaluation
Purpose: assess solution performance and the value it delivers.

list.item: Measure Solution Performance — define how performance is measured; output: performance measures.
Analyze Performance Measures — examine data against expected value; output: performance analysis.
Assess Solution Limitations — investigate problems and causes; output: solution limitations.
Assess Enterprise Limitations — investigate enterprise factor impacts.
Recommend Actions — identify actions to increase value; output: value improvement recommendations.

section.p: Linked methodologies: solution-assessment. Outputs: performance metrics, value realization report, improvement recommendations, lessons learned.

section.name: classification-and-perspectives
section.p: Requirements Classification, Perspectives, and Usage by Phase

section.name: requirements-classification
section.p: Requirements Classification
BABOK classifies requirements into four types based on their role in the change initiative:

list.item: Business requirements — WHY the change is needed. Example: increase market share by 10%.
Stakeholder requirements — WHAT users and stakeholders need. Example: faster order processing.
Solution requirements — WHAT the system does (functional and non-functional). Example: calculate shipping cost in under 2 seconds.
Transition requirements — HOW to get from current to future state. Example: migrate 3 years of order history.

section.name: babok-perspectives
section.p: BABOK Perspectives
Five perspectives adapt the six knowledge areas to specific delivery contexts. Declare one perspective explicitly at project start — leaving it implicit produces inconsistent output across agents.

list.item: Agile — iterative, collaborative; extends to scaled frameworks (SAFe, LeSS) and DevOps.
Business Intelligence — data analysis and reporting; expanded to AI/ML and advanced analytics.
Information Technology — systems and technology; cloud-native, microservices, API-first.
Business Architecture — enterprise-level design; digital transformation and platform thinking.
Business Process Management — process optimization; intelligent automation and process mining.

rule.statement: Pair the overview with one declared perspective before any agent generates BA deliverables.
rule.rationale: Without explicit perspective, models default to IT-perspective regardless of actual need; a BPM initiative gets system requirements when the user wanted process improvements.

section.name: underlying-competencies
section.p: Underlying Competencies
Skills required for effective BA work across all knowledge areas:

list.item: Analytical — creative thinking, decision making, systems thinking, problem solving.
Behavioral — ethics, accountability, trustworthiness, adaptability.
Business — business acumen, industry knowledge, organization knowledge.
Communication — verbal, written, listening, non-verbal.
Interaction — facilitation, leadership, teamwork, negotiation.
Tools and Technology — modeling tools, documentation platforms, automation tools.

section.name: knowledge-area-usage-by-project-phase
section.p: Knowledge Area Usage by Project Phase
Match situation to primary knowledge area focus:

list.item: New project starting → Planning, Strategy Analysis.
Gathering requirements → Elicitation, Requirements Analysis.
Managing changes → Lifecycle Management.
System implementation → Analysis, Solution Evaluation.
Post go-live → Solution Evaluation.
Process improvement → Strategy Analysis, Requirements Analysis.

rule.statement: For agile projects, collapse Planning and Strategy into a single Sprint 0 deliverable. The BABOK linear flow is wrong for iterative teams.
rule.rationale: Treating BABOK as a rigid sequential process on agile projects produces waterfall artifacts with sprint-shaped naming; compress and adapt.

antipattern.description: Skipping knowledge areas because "this is a small project".
antipattern.reason: Small projects skip Planning (no stakeholder map) and Evaluation (no feedback loop); both failures produce avoidable rework in subsequent iterations.

antipattern.description: Using BABOK as a rigid process instead of a competency and knowledge framework.
antipattern.reason: BABOK is descriptive, not prescriptive. Applying it linearly on agile projects produces waterfall artifacts even when the team is running sprints.

template.purpose: Per-project checklist tracking applicable KAs, deliverable status, and location.
Business Analysis Plan template covering scope, KA approach, and resources.

section.name: agentic-workflow-and-integration
section.p: Treat the 6 KAs as a routing tree, not a sequential pipeline. A planner agent classifies the inbound request into one or more KAs, then dispatches per-KA subagents in parallel where dependencies allow. Always commit the KA classification to a memory file before dispatch — downstream agents read it instead of re-classifying. End every run with a KA-3 trace pass so artifacts stay linked.

This overview is an index-level integration guide. It does not duplicate per-methodology agent files (those live next to each named methodology under business-analyst/, ba-core/, ba-modeling/). Use it to pick which KA — and which downstream methodology — an agent should run.

rule.statement: Classify every BA request into one or more KAs before generating any deliverable. Use the KA classification result as shared state that all downstream agents read — never re-classify per task.
rule.rationale: Skipping the classification step causes agents to draft KA-5 specs when the actual gap is KA-1 stakeholder mapping; re-classifying per task produces inconsistent results across the pipeline.

section.name: implementation-considerations
section.p: BABOK is artifact-heavy. LLMs over-produce structure (10-page docs for a 3-feature MVP). Force scope before generation.
KA-3 (Lifecycle) requires a system of record (Jira/Linear/ADO). An agent without write access can only emit drafts; traceability matrices stay stale unless wired to the issue tracker.
KA-4 (Strategy) needs ground truth about current state (org chart, KPIs, capability map). LLMs hallucinate plausible-but-wrong baselines without a document source.
KA-6 (Solution Evaluation) needs telemetry. No metrics → agent invents metrics. Block this branch unless an analytics datasource is attached.
The 6-KA framing is BABOK v3 (waterfall-leaning). For pure agile/discovery, prefer pm-agile/ and product-manager/ skills instead.
LLMs default to KA-5 (modelling) on any input. Fight this — most real failures are KA-1 (no stakeholder map) or KA-3 (no traceability). Make the planner explicitly score all 6 KAs before dispatch.
Agents will fabricate "industry standard" KPIs in KA-6. Require a citation or a datasource path for every metric; reject otherwise.

BABOK terminology collides with agile terminology (e.g. "requirement" vs "story", "stakeholder" vs "persona"). Pin a glossary in the agent's system prompt — drift causes traceability errors when artifacts cross teams.

Verification (KA-5) is distinct from Validation (KA-5). Two separate tasks. Do not let one prompt produce both — they require different mental modes (quality vs business fit) and a single pass usually does neither well.
Multi-agent runs across KAs duplicate stakeholder lists. Centralise stakeholder register in one file referenced by all KA agents — never let each agent maintain its own copy.
KA-3 traceability matrices require stable IDs. If your agent assigns IDs at generation time, they renumber on every run. Use content-hash IDs or pull IDs from the issue tracker before drafting.

section.name: best-practices-for-implementation
section.p: Classify before generating. Skipping the KA-routing step makes agents draft KA-5 specs when the actual gap is KA-1 stakeholder mapping. This is the single most common failure mode in BA automation.
Persist the classification (e.g. in .aidocs/ba-routing.json) so re-runs converge instead of reclassifying and producing inconsistent guidance.
For KA-3, never let an agent both author and approve a requirement in the same run — split into author and reviewer agents with different prompts to maintain separation of concerns.
KA-2 and KA-5 should always emit machine-readable artifacts (JSON requirement objects, BPMN XML, Gherkin) alongside the human prose. Prose-only output breaks downstream traceability.

Use the BABOK competency table (analytical/behavioural/business/communication/interaction) as a tag set for prompt routing — e.g. tasks tagged interaction:facilitation go to the faion-brainstorm skill rather than a solo agent.
Cap each KA artifact to a hard line budget (KA-1 plan ≤ 200 lines, KA-4 strategy ≤ 300, KA-5 spec ≤ 500 per epic). Without caps LLMs balloon docs into unusable proportions.
